analysis.note: Adjectival genitive singular agreeing with svabhāvasya: “of the threefold ...”.

verification.detail: compound stem trividha as Pum/Sasthi/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Genitive singular governed semantically by niḥsvabhāvatām: the threefold absence-of-nature is “of the threefold nature.”

verification.detail: compound stem svabhāva as Pum/Sasthi/Eka: pass; relation tatpurusa: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Feminine accusative singular agreeing with niḥsvabhāvatām.

verification.detail: compound stem trividha as Stri/Dvitiya/Eka: pass; relation bahuvrihi: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Abstract feminine noun in -tā from niḥsvabhāva, “state/condition of being without svabhāva”; accusative object of sandhāya.
Absolutive/gerund: saṃ-√dhā + -ya, here construed with the accusative niḥsvabhāvatām: “having in view / with reference to.”
Genitive plural compound dependent on niḥsvabhāvatā: “of all dharmas.”

verification.detail: compound stem sarvadharma as Pum/Sasthi/Bahu: pass; relation karmadharaya: unsupported (logged)

analysis.note: Past passive participial adjective, feminine nominative singular, agreeing with niḥsvabhāvatā: “taught/pointed out.”
Nominative singular subject of deśitā: “absence of nature / selflessness.”

justifications.decision: trividhasya is an adjective in the genitive singular agreeing with masculine svabhāvasya, not a separate substantive genitive. The phrase supplies the genitive complement to trividhām niḥsvabhāvatām.
options_considered: take trividhasya substantively: “of the threefold one”
take trividhasya adjectivally with svabhāvasya: “of the threefold nature”

evidence.quote: MW s.v. trividha: “mfn. of 3 kinds, triple, threefold”; MW s.v. svabhāva: “own condition or state of being ... nature.”
evidence.translation: Lexically and morphologically, the phrase is “of the threefold nature.”

justifications.decision: trividhām and niḥsvabhāvatām are both feminine accusative singular, so trividhām modifies niḥsvabhāvatām. niḥsvabhāvatām is best analyzed as the accusative singular of abstract niḥsvabhāvatā, not as niḥsvabhāva plus an independent tām.
options_considered: niḥsvabhāva + tām as a compound or pronoun-like sequence
niḥsvabhāvatā as one abstract feminine noun in -tā
justifications.chosen: trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām = “the threefold absence of nature”

evidence.quote: MW s.v. niḥsvabhāva: “mfn. ... void of peculiarities”; Sanskrit -tā forms feminine abstract nouns, with acc. sg. -tām and nom. sg. -tā.
evidence.translation: The form means “absence/state of being without svabhāva,” and trividhām makes that absence threefold.

justifications.decision: The absolutive sandhāya is construed with the accusative trividhāṃ niḥsvabhāvatām. In this abstract context, a literal physical “joining” is less apt than “holding/fixing in view,” hence “having in view” or “with reference to.”
options_considered: contextual abstract “having in view / with reference to”
justifications.chosen: sandhāya = “having in view / with reference to”

evidence.quote: MW s.v. saṃ-√dhā: “to place or hold or put or draw or join or fasten or fix ... unite.”
evidence.translation: The core sense “hold/fix” supports the contextual rendering “having in view.”

justifications.decision: sarvadharmāṇām is genitive plural and most naturally supplies the possessor/objective genitive for the nominative niḥsvabhāvatā: “the absence of nature of all dharmas.”
options_considered: take sarvadharmāṇām with deśitā as an indirect genitive of agent or audience
take sarvadharmāṇām as the genitive complement of niḥsvabhāvatā

evidence.quote: MW s.v. sarva: “all, every”; MW s.v. dharma: “that which is established or firm ... law ... duty.”
evidence.translation: The compound sarvadharma is “all dharmas,” and the genitive plural gives “of all dharmas.”

justifications.decision: deśitā is feminine nominative singular and agrees with niḥsvabhāvatā, the nominative subject. As a past passive participial predicate, it yields “has been taught / was taught.”
options_considered: take deśitā as modifying an omitted feminine noun
take deśitā predicatively with niḥsvabhāvatā as its subject
justifications.chosen: deśitā niḥsvabhāvatā = “the absence of nature has been taught”

evidence.quote: The endings -ā ... -tā are feminine nominative singular in deśitā niḥsvabhāvatā; the participial form deśita- functions adjectivally as “taught/pointed out.”
evidence.translation: The predicate construction is “the absence of nature was taught.”

translation:
Having in view the threefold absence of nature (niḥsvabhāvatā)
of the threefold nature (svabhāva),
the absence of nature of all dharmas
has been taught.
one_shot_delta: A grammar-only or analyzer-led reading could wrongly split niḥsvabhāvatām/niḥsvabhāvatā into niḥsvabhāva + tām/tā instead of recognizing the abstract noun niḥsvabhāvatā.
It could translate sandhāya too literally as “having joined,” whereas the abstract accusative favors “having in view / with reference to.”
It could fail to keep the two “threefold” expressions aligned: trividhasya qualifies svabhāvasya, while trividhām qualifies niḥsvabhāvatām.
Without commentary, the verse itself does not identify which three natures or which three absences of nature are meant; the translation therefore remains generic.
It could render dharmāṇām as “laws/duties” from a general dictionary sense; preserving “dharmas” avoids over-specifying the technical sense without commentary.
analyzer_disagreements: The analyzer splits niḥsvabhāvatām and niḥsvabhāvatā as niḥsvabhāva_Cp + tām/tā. I instead analyze each as the single abstract feminine noun-stem niḥsvabhāvatā, because -tā here is the regular abstract-forming suffix, not an independent noun/pronoun.

The analyzer gives sandhāya with lemma saṃdhā. I normalize this to bare root √dhā with prefix sam, per the requested format for verbal derivation, while retaining its indeclinable absolutive function.
The analyzer treats sarvadharmāṇām by segmenting sarva_Cp + dharmāṇām. I record the inflected nominal stem as the whole compound sarvadharma, as requested for inflected compounds.
The analyzer leaves trividha unsplit. I add a compound analysis tri + vidha, taking the lexical adjective “threefold” as a bahuvrīhi-type formation; this does not change its inflectional analysis.
open_questions: The verse alone does not specify the members of the “threefold nature” or the “threefold absence of nature.”
The exact doctrinal scope of niḥsvabhāvatā cannot be fixed from the supplied grammar and dictionary alone.
The agent of deśitā, presumably an authoritative teacher, is not expressed in the verse.
